techniques.evidence_quote: two former workers at its e-commerce provider GoQuo in a development center in India 'improperly accessed and stole the personal data of our customers.'
two former employees of a third-party provider were responsible for a massive breach
Initial reports suggested a misconfigured Amazon Web Services (AWS) S3 bucket may have been to blame for the security incident
improperly accessed and stole the personal data of our customers
The records — which contained names, dates of birth, phone numbers, emails, addresses, passport numbers and expiration dates — were spotted circulating on the dark web

attack_chain.description: Two former employees of third-party e-commerce provider GoQuo (India development center) leveraged their existing or residual access credentials to authenticate to Malindo Air's systems after their employment ended.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: two former workers at its e-commerce provider GoQuo in a development center in India 'improperly accessed and stole the personal data of our customers.'

attack_chain.description: The former employees accessed cloud-hosted storage (suspected AWS S3 bucket) containing approximately 35 million passenger PII records including names, dates of birth, phone numbers, emails, addresses, passport numbers and expiration dates.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Initial reports suggested a misconfigured Amazon Web Services (AWS) S3 bucket may have been to blame for the security incident

attack_chain.description: The stolen 35 million PII records were exfiltrated and subsequently made available on the dark web, indicating data was transferred out of Malindo Air's environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The records … were spotted circulating on the dark web last month

initial_access_vector: Former employees of third-party e-commerce provider GoQuo used their (likely residual/retained) legitimate credentials to improperly access Malindo Air customer data systems after leaving the company.
